1→# Module Standards
     2→
     3→**Purpose:** Defines what a BMAD module is, its structure, and the three types of modules.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## What is a BMAD Module?
     8→
     9→A **BMAD module** is a self-contained package of functionality that extends the BMAD framework. Modules provide:
    10→- **Agents** — AI personas with specialized expertise and menu-driven commands
    11→- **Workflows** — Structured processes for accomplishing complex tasks
    12→- **Configuration** — module.yaml for user customization
    13→
    14→---
    15→
    16→## Module Types
    17→
    18→### 1. Standalone Module
    19→
    20→A new, independent module focused on a specific domain.
    21→
    22→**Characteristics:**
    23→- Own module code (e.g., `healthcare-ai`, `legal-assist`)
    24→- Independent of other modules
    25→- Can be installed alongside any other modules
    26→- Has its own agents, workflows, configuration
    27→
    28→**Location:** `src/modules/{module-code}/`
    29→
    30→**Example:** CIS (Creative Innovation Suite) — a standalone module for innovation workflows
    31→
    32→---
    33→
    34→### 2. Extension Module
    35→
    36→Extends an existing BMAD module with additional functionality.
    37→
    38→**Characteristics:**
    39→- Builds upon an existing module's agents and workflows
    40→- May add new agents or workflows that complement the base module
    41→- Shares configuration context with the extended module
    42→- Typically installed alongside the module it extends
    43→
    44→**Location:** `src/modules/{base-module}/extensions/{extension-code}/`
    45→
    46→**Example:** An extension to BMM that adds specialized security review workflows
    47→
    48→---
    49→
    50→### Extension Module: Override & Merge Pattern
    51→
    52→When an extension module is installed, its files merge with the base module following these rules:
    53→
    54→#### Code Matching
    55→
    56→The extension's `module.yaml` `code:` field matches the base module's code:
    57→
    58→```yaml
    59→# Base module: src/modules/bmm/module.yaml
    60→code: bmm
    61→
    62→# Extension: src/modules/bmm/extensions/security/module.yaml
    63→code: bmm  # SAME CODE — extends BMM
    64→```
    65→
    66→The **folder name** is unique (e.g., `bmm-security`) but the `code:` matches the base module.
    67→
    68→#### File Merge Rules
    69→
    70→| File Type | Same Name | Different Name |
    71→|-----------|-----------|----------------|
    72→| Agent file | **OVERRIDE** — replaces the base agent | **ADD** — new agent added |
    73→| Workflow folder | **OVERRIDE** — replaces the base workflow | **ADD** — new workflow added |
    74→| Other files | **OVERRIDE** — replaces base file | **ADD** — new file added |
    75→
    76→#### Examples
    77→
    78→**Override scenario:**
    79→```
    80→Base module (BMM):
    81→├── agents/
    82→│   └── pm.agent.yaml          # Original PM agent
    83→
    84→Extension (bmm-security):
    85→├── agents/
    86→│   └── pm.agent.yaml          # Security-focused PM — REPLACES original
    87→
    88→Result after installation:
    89→├── agents/
    90→│   └── pm.agent.yaml          # Now the security version
    91→```
    92→
    93→**Add scenario:**
    94→```
    95→Base module (BMM):
    96→├── agents/
    97→│   ├── pm.agent.yaml
    98→│   └── architect.agent.yaml
    99→
   100→Extension (bmm-security):
   101→├── agents/
   102→│   └── security-auditor.agent.yaml  # NEW agent
   103→
   104→Result after installation:
   105→├── agents/
   106→│   ├── pm.agent.yaml
   107→│   ├── architect.agent.yaml
   108→│   └── security-auditor.agent.yaml  # ADDED
   109→```
   110→
   111→**Mixed scenario:**
   112→```
   113→Extension contains both overrides and new files — applies rules per file
   114→```
   115→
   116→---
   117→
   118→### 3. Global Module
   119→
   120→Affects the entire BMAD framework and all modules.
   121→
   122→**Characteristics:**
   123→- Core functionality that impacts all modules
   124→- Often provides foundational services or utilities
   125→- Installed at the framework level
   126→- Use sparingly — only for truly global concerns
   127→
   128→**Location:** `src/modules/{module-code}/` with `global: true` in module.yaml
   129→
   130→**Example:** A module that provides universal logging or telemetry across BMAD
   131→
   132→---
   133→
   134→## Required Module Structure
   135→
   136→```
   137→{module-code}/
   138→├── module.yaml                 # Module configuration (REQUIRED)
   139→├── README.md                   # Module documentation (REQUIRED)
   140→├── agents/                     # Agent definitions (if any)
   141→│   └── {agent-name}.agent.yaml
   142→├── workflows/                  # Workflow definitions (if any)
   143→│   └── {workflow-name}/
   144→│       └── workflow.md
   145→└── {other folders}             # Tasks, templates, data as needed
   146→```
   147→
   148→---
   149→
   150→## Required Files
   151→
   152→### module.yaml (REQUIRED)
   153→
   154→Every module MUST have a `module.yaml` file with at minimum:
   155→
   156→```yaml
   157→code: {module-code}
   158→name: "Module Display Name"
   159→header: "Brief module description"
   160→subheader: "Additional context"
   161→default_selected: false
   162→```
   163→
   164→See: `module-yaml-conventions.md` for full specification.
   165→
   166→---
   167→
   168→### README.md (REQUIRED)
   169→
   170→Every module MUST have a README.md with:
   171→- Module name and purpose
   172→- Installation instructions
   173→- Components section (agents, workflows)
   174→- Quick start guide
   175→- Module structure diagram
   176→- Configuration section
   177→- Usage examples
   178→- Author information
   179→
   180→---
   181→
   182→## Optional Components
   183→
   184→### Agents
   185→
   186→Agents are AI personas with:
   187→- Metadata (id, name, title, icon, module)
   188→- Persona (role, identity, communication_style, principles)
   189→- Menu (trigger → workflow/exec mappings)
   190→
   191→See: `agent-architecture.md` for design guidance.
   192→
   193→---
   194→
   195→### Workflows
   196→
   197→Workflows are structured processes with:
   198→- workflow.md (entry point)
   199→- steps/ folder with step files
   200→- data/ folder with shared reference
   201→- templates/ folder if needed
   202→
   203→---
   204→
   205→---
   206→
   207→## Module Type Decision Tree
   208→
   209→```
   210→START: Creating a module
   211→│
   212→├─ Is this a brand new independent domain?
   213→│  └─ YES → Standalone Module
   214→│
   215→├─ Does this extend an existing module?
   216→│  └─ YES → Extension Module
   217→│
   218→└─ Does this affect all modules globally?
   219→   └─ YES → Global Module (use sparingly)
   220→```
   221→
   222→---
   223→
   224→## Naming Conventions
   225→
   226→### Module Code
   227→
   228→- **kebab-case** (e.g., `bmm`, `cis`, `bmgd`, `healthcare-ai`)
   229→- Short, memorable, descriptive
   230→- 2-20 characters
   231→- Lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens only
   232→
   233→### Agent Files
   234→
   235→- Format: `{role-name}.agent.yaml`
   236→- Example: `pm.agent.yaml`, `architect.agent.yaml`
   237→
   238→### Workflow Folders
   239→
   240→- Format: `{workflow-name}/`
   241→- Example: `prd/`, `create-architecture/`
   242→
   243→---
   244→
   245→## Module Dependencies
   246→
   247→Modules can depend on:
   248→- **Core BMAD** — Always available
   249→- **Other modules** — Specify in module.yaml as `dependencies:`
   250→- **External tools** — Document in README
   251→
   252→---
   253→
   254→## Quick Reference
   255→
   256→| Question | Answer |
   257→|----------|--------|
   258→| What's a module? | Self-contained package of agents, workflows, config |
   259→| What are the types? | Standalone, Extension, Global |
   260→| What's required? | module.yaml, README.md |
   261→| Where do modules live? | `src/modules/{code}/` |
   262→| How do agents work? | Menu triggers → workflow/exec |
   263→| How does installation work? | module.yaml prompts |
   264→

1→# module.yaml Conventions
     2→
     3→**Purpose:** Defines how module.yaml works, including variables, templates, and how they provide context to agents and workflows.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## Overview
     8→
     9→`module.yaml` is the configuration file for a BMAD module. It:
    10→- Defines module metadata (code, name, description)
    11→- Collects user input via prompts during installation
    12→- Makes those inputs available to agents and workflows as variables
    13→- Specifies which module should be selected by default
    14→
    15→---
    16→
    17→## Frontmatter Fields
    18→
    19→### Required Fields
    20→
    21→```yaml
    22→code: {module-code}              # kebab-case identifier
    23→name: "Display Name"             # Human-readable name
    24→header: "Brief description"      # One-line summary
    25→subheader: "Additional context"  # More detail
    26→default_selected: false          # Auto-select on install?
    27→```
    28→
    29→### `default_selected` Guidelines
    30→
    31→| Module Type | default_selected | Example |
    32→|-------------|------------------|---------|
    33→| Core/Primary | `true` | BMM (agile software delivery) |
    34→| Specialized | `false` | CIS (creative innovation), BMGD (game dev) |
    35→| Experimental | `false` | New modules in development |
    36→
    37→---
    38→
    39→## Variables System
    40→
    41→### Core Config Variables (Always Available)
    42→
    43→These variables are automatically available to ALL modules:
    44→
    45→```yaml
    46→# Variables from Core Config inserted:
    47→## user_name                  # User's name
    48→## communication_language     # Preferred language
    49→## document_output_language    # Output document language
    50→## output_folder             # Default output location
    51→```
    52→
    53→No need to define these — they're injected automatically.
    54→
    55→---
    56→
    57→### Custom Variables
    58→
    59→Define custom variables for user input:
    60→
    61→```yaml
    62→variable_name:
    63→  prompt: "Question to ask the user?"
    64→  default: "{default_value}"
    65→  result: "{template_for_final_value}"
    66→```
    67→
    68→**Example:**
    69→
    70→```yaml
    71→project_name:
    72→  prompt: "What is the title of your project?"
    73→  default: "{directory_name}"
    74→  result: "{value}"
    75→```
    76→
    77→### Variable Templates
    78→
    79→In `prompt` and `result`, you can use templates:
    80→
    81→| Template | Expands To |
    82→|----------|------------|
    83→| `{value}` | The user's input |
    84→| `{directory_name}` | Current directory name |
    85→| `{output_folder}` | Output folder from core config |
    86→| `{project-root}` | Project root path |
    87→| `{variable_name}` | Another variable's value |
    88→
    89→---
    90→
    91→## Variable Types
    92→
    93→### 1. Simple Text Input
    94→
    95→```yaml
    96→project_name:
    97→  prompt: "What is the title of your project?"
    98→  default: "{directory_name}"
    99→  result: "{value}"
   100→```
   101→
   102→---
   103→
   104→### 2. Boolean/Flag
   105→
   106→```yaml
   107→enable_feature:
   108→  prompt: "Enable this feature?"
   109→  default: false
   110→  result: "{value}"
   111→```
   112→
   113→---
   114→
   115→### 3. Single Select
   116→
   117→```yaml
   118→skill_level:
   119→  prompt: "What is your experience level?"
   120→  default: "intermediate"
   121→  result: "{value}"
   122→  single-select:
   123→    - value: "beginner"
   124→      label: "Beginner - Explains concepts clearly"
   125→    - value: "intermediate"
   126→      label: "Intermediate - Balanced approach"
   127→    - value: "expert"
   128→      label: "Expert - Direct and technical"
   129→```
   130→
   131→---
   132→
   133→### 4. Multi Select
   134→
   135→```yaml
   136→platforms:
   137→  prompt: "Which platforms do you need?"
   138→  default: ["unity", "unreal"]
   139→  result: "{value}"
   140→  multi-select:
   141→    - value: "unity"
   142→      label: "Unity"
   143→    - value: "unreal"
   144→      label: "Unreal Engine"
   145→    - value: "godot"
   146→      label: "Godot"
   147→```
   148→
   149→---
   150→
   151→### 5. Multi-Line Prompt
   152→
   153→```yaml
   154→complex_variable:
   155→  prompt:
   156→    - "First question?"
   157→    - "Second context?"
   158→    - "Third detail?"
   159→  default: "default_value"
   160→  result: "{value}"
   161→```
   162→
   163→---
   164→
   165→### 6. Required Variable
   166→
   167→```yaml
   168→critical_variable:
   169→  prompt: "Required information:"
   170→  required: true
   171→  result: "{value}"
   172→```
   173→
   174→---
   175→
   176→### 7. Path Variable
   177→
   178→```yaml
   179→artifacts_folder:
   180→  prompt: "Where should artifacts be stored?"
   181→  default: "{output_folder}/artifacts"
   182→  result: "{project-root}/{value}"
   183→```
   184→
   185→---
   186→
   187→## Variable Inheritance / Aliasing
   188→
   189→Create an alias for another variable:
   190→
   191→```yaml
   192→primary_artifacts:
   193→  prompt: "Where should primary artifacts be stored?"
   194→  default: "{output_folder}/artifacts"
   195→  result: "{project-root}/{value}"
   196→
   197→# Alias for workflow compatibility
   198→sprint_artifacts:
   199→  inherit: "primary_artifacts"
   200→```
   201→
   202→Now `sprint_artifacts` and `primary_artifacts` reference the same value.
   203→
   204→---
   205→
   206→## How Variables Become Available
   207→
   208→### To Agents
   209→
   210→After installation, variables are available in agent frontmatter/context:
   211→
   212→```yaml
   213→# In agent.agent.yaml or workflow execution
   214→{variable_name}  # Expands to the user's configured value
   215→```
   216→
   217→**Example:** If the user configured `project_name: "MyApp"`, agents can reference `{project_name}` and it will expand to `"MyApp"`.
   218→
   219→### To Workflows
   220→
   221→Workflows can reference module variables in their step files:
   222→
   223→```yaml
   224→---
   225→outputFile: '{implementation_artifacts}/my-output.md'
   226→---
   227→```
   228→
   229→This expands the `implementation_artifacts` variable from module.yaml.
   230→
   231→---
   232→
   233→## Real-World Examples
   234→
   235→### BMM (BMad Method) — Complex Configuration
   236→
   237→```yaml
   238→code: bmm
   239→name: "BMM: BMad Method Agile-AI Driven-Development"
   240→header: "BMad Method™: Breakthrough Method of Agile-Ai Driven-Dev"
   241→subheader: "Agent and Workflow Configuration for this module"
   242→default_selected: true
   243→
   244→# Variables from Core Config inserted:
   245→## user_name
   246→## communication_language
   247→## document_output_language
   248→## output_folder
   249→
   250→project_name:
   251→  prompt: "What is the title of your project?"
   252→  default: "{directory_name}"
   253→  result: "{value}"
   254→
   255→user_skill_level:
   256→  prompt:
   257→    - "What is your development experience level?"
   258→    - "This affects how agents explain concepts."
   259→  default: "intermediate"
   260→  result: "{value}"
   261→  single-select:
   262→    - value: "beginner"
   263→      label: "Beginner - Explain concepts clearly"
   264→    - value: "intermediate"
   265→      label: "Intermediate - Balanced approach"
   266→    - value: "expert"
   267→      label: "Expert - Direct and technical"
   268→
   269→planning_artifacts:
   270→  prompt: "Where should planning artifacts be stored?"
   271→  default: "{output_folder}/planning-artifacts"
   272→  result: "{project-root}/{value}"
   273→
   274→implementation_artifacts:
   275→  prompt: "Where should implementation artifacts be stored?"
   276→  default: "{output_folder}/implementation-artifacts"
   277→  result: "{project-root}/{value}"
   278→
   279→project_knowledge:
   280→  prompt: "Where should project knowledge be stored?"
   281→  default: "docs"
   282→  result: "{project-root}/{value}"
   283→
   284→tea_use_mcp_enhancements:
   285→  prompt: "Enable MCP enhancements in Test Architect?"
   286→  default: false
   287→  result: "{value}"
   288→```
   289→
   290→---
   291→
   292→### CIS (Creative Innovation Suite) — Minimal Configuration
   293→
   294→```yaml
   295→code: cis
   296→name: "CIS: Creative Innovation Suite"
   297→header: "Creative Innovation Suite (CIS) Module"
   298→subheader: "No custom configuration - uses Core settings only"
   299→default_selected: false
   300→
   301→# Variables from Core Config inserted:
   302→## user_name
   303→## communication_language
   304→## document_output_language
   305→## output_folder
   306→```
   307→
   308→Some modules don't need custom variables — core config is enough!
   309→
   310→---
   311→
   312→### BMGD (Game Development) — Multi-Select Example
   313→
   314→```yaml
   315→code: bmgd
   316→name: "BMGD: BMad Game Development"
   317→header: "BMad Game Development Module"
   318→subheader: "Configure game development settings"
   319→default_selected: false
   320→
   321→project_name:
   322→  prompt: "What is the name of your game project?"
   323→  default: "{directory_name}"
   324→  result: "{value}"
   325→
   326→primary_platform:
   327→  prompt: "Which game engine do you use?"
   328→  default: ["unity", "unreal"]
   329→  required: true
   330→  result: "{value}"
   331→  multi-select:
   332→    - value: "unity"
   333→      label: "Unity"
   334→    - value: "unreal"
   335→      label: "Unreal Engine"
   336→    - value: "godot"
   337→      label: "Godot"
   338→    - value: "other"
   339→      label: "Custom / Other"
   340→```
   341→
   342→---
   343→
   344→## Best Practices
   345→
   346→### DO:
   347→- Keep prompts clear and concise
   348→- Provide sensible defaults
   349→- Use `result: "{project-root}/{value}"` for paths
   350→- Use single/multi-select for structured choices
   351→- Group related variables logically
   352→
   353→### DON'T:
   354→- Overwhelm users with too many questions
   355→- Ask for information that could be inferred
   356→- Use technical jargon in prompts
   357→- Create variables that are never used
   358→
   359→---
   360→
   361→## Variable Naming
   362→
   363→- **kebab-case** (e.g., `planning_artifacts`, `user_skill_level`)
   364→- Descriptive but concise
   365→- Avoid conflicts with core variables
   366→
   367→---
   368→
   369→## Testing Your module.yaml
   370→
   371→After creating module.yaml, test it:
   372→
   373→1. Run `bmad install` in a test project
   374→2. Verify prompts appear correctly
   375→3. Check that variables expand in agents/workflows
   376→4. Test default values
   377→5. Validate path templates resolve correctly
   378→
   379→---
   380→
   381→## Quick Reference
   382→
   383→| Pattern | Use Case |
   384→|---------|----------|
   385→| Simple text input | Names, titles, descriptions |
   386→| Boolean/Flag | Enable/disable features |
   387→| Single select | Experience levels, categories |
   388→| Multi select | Platforms, frameworks, options |
   389→| Multi-line prompt | Complex questions needing context |
   390→| Required | Must-have information |
   391→| Path variable | Directory locations |
   392→| Inherit/Alias | Compatibility, references |
   393→

Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile the comprehensive findings.

## Search Results Summary

Based on my thorough analysis of the codebase, here are my findings:

### 1. **`-learn` Directory Generation Pattern**

The `_skf-learn` directory is generated by the installer during installation. Here are the relevant files and implementation details:

**File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/tools/cli/lib/installer.js`**
- Lines 112-122: Step 6 copies learning material
- Lines 294-304: `copyLearningMaterial()` function
  - Creates directory: `_skf-learn` 
  - Copies content from `this.docsDir` (repository `docs/` folder)
  - Can be disabled with `config.install_learning = false`
  
**Code snippet (line 295):**
```javascript
const learnDir = path.join(projectDir, '_skf-learn');
```

### 2. **Pattern Analysis: Is it Always `_{module-code}-learn`?**

**Current Implementation:** The pattern is HARDCODED as `_skf-learn`, NOT dynamic.

**Evidence:**
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/tools/cli/lib/installer.js` (line 295): hardcoded `'_skf-learn'`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/tools/cli/commands/uninstall.js` (line 171): hardcoded `'_skf-learn'`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/tools/cli/lib/manifest.js` (line 54): hardcoded `'_skf-learn'`

**Module Code Reference:**
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/tools/cli/lib/ide-commands.js` (line 203): `const moduleCode = 'skf';`
- The `moduleCode` is only used for IDE command file naming (e.g., `bmad-agent-skf-forger.md`, `bmad-skf-workflow-name.md`)
- The learning directory name is NOT derived from `moduleCode`

**Conclusion:** The pattern is NOT `_{module-code}-learn` but rather a fixed `_skf-learn` specific to the SKF (Skill Forge) module.

### 3. **Other `_*` Prefixed Directories**

Besides `_bmad` and `_skf-learn`, the following `_*` directories are created:

**File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/tools/cli/lib/installer.js`**

| Directory | Purpose | Reference |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| `_bmad/skf/` | Main SKF module installation | Line 24, 57, 295 |
| `_bmad/_memory/forger-sidecar/` | Agent sidecar state (templates) | Lines 195-210 |
| `_bmad/_config/` | Installation manifest storage | manifest.js, line 10 |

**File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/tools/cli/lib/manifest.js`**
- Line 10: `const MANIFEST_DIR = '_bmad/_config'`
- Line 11: `const MANIFEST_FILE = 'skf-manifest.yaml'`

**File: `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/tools/cli/commands/uninstall.js`**
- Line 40: `{ key: 'sidecar', label: 'Agent sidecar state', dir: '_bmad/_memory/forger-sidecar' }`

### 4. **Hardcoded Directory Structure Summary**

```
_bmad/
├── skf/                    # Main SKF module folder (configurable as skfFolder)
├── _config/                # Installation manifest
│   └── skf-manifest.yaml
└── _memory/
    └── forger-sidecar/     # Ferris agent sidecar state

_skf-learn/                 # Learning material (hardcoded, optional)
```

### Key Files with Directory References

1. **`/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/tools/cli/lib/installer.js`**
   - copyLearningMaterial() at line 294-304
   - setupSidecar() at line 194-211
   - updateGitignore() at line 273-292 (adds `_bmad/_memory/` to .gitignore)

2. **`/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/tools/cli/lib/manifest.js`**
   - writeManifest() at line 41-94 (tracks all installed directories)
   - Lines 54-55: hardcoded `_skf-learn` reference

3. **`/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/tools/cli/commands/uninstall.js`**
   - displayRemovalPlan() at line 37-70 (shows directories for removal)
   - Lines 40, 42: hardcoded directory names

4. **`/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/test/test-cli-integration.js`**
   - Lines 121-131: Tests verify `_skf-learn` directory creation
   - Lines 146-147: Tests verify IDE command naming (bmad-agent-skf-*, bmad-skf-*)

- But the user mentioned that "during the installation, some module could come with `_<module-code>-learn` folder" — so they're thinking about future modules too, not just SKF

So the exclusion pattern should be `_*-learn` (glob) to handle any module's learning directory.

Now let me also think about what other `_*` directories might exist:

- `_skf-learn/` — learning material
- Future modules might have `_xyz-learn/`

The user said to exclude `_*-learn` pattern. This is correct and forward-looking.

Now I need to update step-03 with all five changes: skip empty directories before creating collections, exclude `_*-learn` pattern for module learning folders, move `forge-data` to `alwaysExcluded`, clean up stale collections on re-run, and sanitize collection names by stripping the leading `_` from the dirname portion.

input.content:
---
name: 'step-03-auto-index'
description: 'Smart-index user project directories as QMD collections (Deep tier only)'

nextStepFile: './step-04-report.md'

# Directories ALWAYS excluded regardless of git status — module internals and forge artifacts
alwaysExcluded:
  - _bmad
  - forge-data

# Directory name patterns ALWAYS excluded (glob-style matching)
# _*-learn: module learning material directories (e.g., _skf-learn, _xyz-learn)
alwaysExcludedPatterns:
  - '_*-learn'

# Fallback exclusion list for non-git projects only
# Used ONLY when the project is not a git repository
fallbackExcludedDirs:
  - _bmad
  - forge-data
  - node_modules
  - .git
  - dist
  - build
  - coverage
  - __pycache__
  - target
  - .next
  - .nuxt
  - .cache
  - vendor
  - out
  - .turbo
  - venv
  - .venv
  - .output
---

# Step 3: Smart Auto-Index Project

## STEP GOAL:

If the detected tier is Deep, discover **user project directories** and index each as a targeted QMD collection. Uses `git ls-files` as the authoritative source for what belongs to the project (respects all `.gitignore` rules), with a hardcoded fallback for non-git projects.

Directories in `{alwaysExcluded}` and patterns in `{alwaysExcludedPatterns}` are excluded regardless of git status. Empty directories are skipped. Stale collections from prior runs are cleaned up.

For Quick and Forge tiers, skip silently and proceed.

## MANDATORY EXECUTION RULES (READ FIRST):

### Universal Rules:

- 📖 CRITICAL: Read the complete step file before taking any action
- 🔄 CRITICAL: When loading next step, ensure entire file is read
- 🎯 Execute all operations autonomously — no user interaction

### Role Reinforcement:

- ✅ You are a system executor performing smart conditional indexing
- ✅ Graceful degradation is paramount — never fail the workflow over indexing
- ✅ No negative messaging — do not mention what non-Deep tiers are missing

### Step-Specific Rules:

- 🎯 Focus only on targeted QMD indexing (Deep tier) or graceful skip (other tiers)
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to display "missing" or "skipped" messages for non-Deep tiers
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to fail the workflow if QMD indexing encounters errors
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to index directories matching `{alwaysExcluded}` or `{alwaysExcludedPatterns}`
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to index the entire project root as a single collection
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to create collections for empty directories
- 💬 If indexing fails: log the issue, note that index can be retried, continue

## EXECUTION PROTOCOLS:

- 🎯 Follow the MANDATORY SEQUENCE exactly
- 💾 QMD indexing targets only user project directories — never module internals
- 📖 Use {calculated_tier} from step-01 context
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to attempt indexing for Quick or Forge tiers
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to use `**/*.md` as the mask — use `**/*` to capture all file types (source code, docs, configs)

## CONTEXT BOUNDARIES:

- Available: {calculated_tier} from step-01, forge-tier.yaml written in step-02
- Available: {project_name} from workflow config
- Focus: conditional QMD indexing only
- Limits: only index if Deep tier AND user content exists
- Dependencies: step-02 must have completed (forge-tier.yaml exists)

## MANDATORY SEQUENCE

**CRITICAL:** Follow this sequence exactly. Do not skip, reorder, or improvise.

### 1. Check Tier

Read `{calculated_tier}` from context.

**If tier is NOT Deep:** Proceed directly to section 7 (Auto-Proceed) — no output, no messaging.

**If tier IS Deep:** Continue to section 2.

### 2. Discover User Project Directories

Determine which top-level directories contain user project content that should be indexed.

#### 2a. Check if project is a git repository

Run: `git -C {project-root} rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree 2>/dev/null`

**If git repo (exit code 0):** Use the **git-aware strategy** (section 2b).

**If NOT a git repo:** Use the **fallback strategy** (section 2c).

#### 2b. Git-Aware Strategy (preferred)

Use `git ls-files` to discover which top-level directories contain tracked content. This automatically respects ALL `.gitignore` rules (project-level, nested, global, and `.git/info/exclude`).

Run:
```bash
cd {project-root} && git ls-files | cut -d/ -f1 | sort -u
```

This produces a list of top-level entries (files and directories) that git tracks.

**Filter the results:**
- Keep only **directories** (ignore root-level files — the agent can read those directly)
- Remove all directories matching `{alwaysExcluded}` (exact match)
- Remove all directories matching `{alwaysExcludedPatterns}` (glob match — e.g., `_*-learn` matches `_skf-learn`, `_xyz-learn`)

**Store the result as `{user_dirs}`.**

#### 2c. Fallback Strategy (non-git projects)

List top-level directories in the project root:

Run: `ls -d {project-root}/*/ 2>/dev/null`

**Filter the results:**
- Remove all directories listed in `{fallbackExcludedDirs}` (exact match)
- Remove all directories matching `{alwaysExcludedPatterns}` (glob match)
- Remove all dot-directories (any directory starting with `.`)

**Store the result as `{user_dirs}`.**

### 3. Filter Empty Directories

For each directory in `{user_dirs}`, check if it contains any files:

Run: `find {dir_path} -type f -maxdepth 3 | head -1`

- If output is empty (no files found within 3 levels): **remove from `{user_dirs}`**
- If output has a result: keep in `{user_dirs}`

This prevents creating QMD collections for empty directories.

### 4. Evaluate Indexability

**If `{user_dirs}` is empty** (no user content directories with files found):

- Store in context: `{qmd_indexed: false, qmd_skip_reason: "no_project_content"}`
- Note for step-04: "No project source directories found to index. QMD collections will be created automatically on next [SF] run after adding project content."
- Proceed directly to section 7 (Auto-Proceed)

**If `{user_dirs}` has entries:** Continue to section 5.

### 5. Clean Up Stale Collections (Re-run Only)

Check for existing QMD collections that belong to this project but no longer map to a valid directory.

Run: `qmd collection list` and identify collections whose name starts with `{project_name}-`.

For each such collection:
- Extract the directory name from the collection name (the part after `{project_name}-`)
- Check if that directory still exists in `{user_dirs}`
- If NOT found in `{user_dirs}`: remove the stale collection with `qmd collection remove {collection_name}`
- If found: it will be updated in section 6

**Error handling:** If collection listing or removal fails, log and continue — stale cleanup is best-effort.

### 6. Index User Directories with QMD (Deep Tier Only)

For **each directory** in `{user_dirs}`, create a QMD collection:

- **Collection name**: `{project_name}-{sanitized_dirname}`
  - Sanitize the dirname: strip any leading underscores or dots (e.g., `_src` → `src`, `.config` → `config`)
  - Examples: `myapp-src`, `myapp-docs`, `myapp-lib`
- **Path**: the full absolute path to the directory
- **Mask**: `**/*` (captures all file types — source code, markdown, configs, etc.)

**Command per directory:**
```bash
qmd collection add {dir_path} --name {project_name}-{sanitized_dirname} --mask "**/*"
```

**Re-run handling:** If QMD reports "already exists" for a collection:
- Do NOT fail — this means it was indexed on a prior run
- Run `qmd update` once at the end to re-index all existing collections with fresh content
- Note the collection as "updated" rather than "created"

**After all collections are processed:**
- Store in context: `{qmd_indexed: true, qmd_collections: [list of names], qmd_total_files: total}`
- Count total files indexed across all collections from QMD output

**Timeout handling:** If indexing takes excessively long on a large project:
- Log that indexing is in progress but may need more time
- Note in context that index may be incomplete
- Do NOT halt or fail the workflow

**Error handling:** If QMD indexing fails for a specific directory:
- Log the specific error for that directory
- Continue with remaining directories — do not abort the loop
- Note which collections succeeded and which failed

If ALL directories fail:
- Store in context: `{qmd_indexed: false, qmd_skip_reason: "all_indexing_failed"}`
- Note that indexing can be retried by re-running [SF]
- The forge-tier.yaml already records `qmd: true` — the tool is available even if indexing failed

### 7. Auto-Proceed

"**Proceeding to forge status report...**"

#### Menu Handling Logic:

- After indexing completes (or is skipped), immediately load, read entire file, then execute {nextStepFile}

#### EXECUTION RULES:

- This is an auto-proceed step with no user choices
- Proceed directly to next step after indexing or skip

## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE

ONLY WHEN the tier check has been performed (and indexing completed or skipped accordingly) will you load and read fully `{nextStepFile}` to execute the report step.

---

## 🚨 SYSTEM SUCCESS/FAILURE METRICS

### ✅ SUCCESS:

- Git repo: `git ls-files` used to discover indexable directories (respects all .gitignore rules)
- Non-git: fallback exclusion list applied correctly
- `{alwaysExcluded}` directories excluded in ALL cases (git or non-git)
- `{alwaysExcludedPatterns}` patterns excluded in ALL cases (e.g., `_skf-learn` matched by `_*-learn`)
- Empty directories skipped — no collections created for dirs with no files
- Stale collections from prior runs detected and removed
- Collection names sanitized (leading `_` and `.` stripped from dirname portion)
- Deep tier with user content: each user directory indexed as its own QMD collection with `**/*` mask
- Deep tier with no user content: skipped with informative note for step-04
- Deep tier re-run: stale collections removed, existing updated via `qmd update`, new directories added
- Quick/Forge tier: skipped silently with no negative messaging
- Workflow continues regardless of indexing outcome
- Auto-proceeded to step-04

### ❌ SYSTEM FAILURE:

- Indexing `_bmad/` or `forge-data/` under any circumstance
- Indexing directories matching `_*-learn` pattern
- Creating collections for empty directories
- Using `**/*.md` mask instead of `**/*` (misses source code files critical for skill generation)
- Indexing the entire project root as a single collection (pollutes results with module internals)
- Not using `git ls-files` when the project IS a git repo
- Leaving stale collections from deleted directories on re-run
- Attempting QMD indexing for Quick or Forge tiers
- Displaying "skipped" or "missing" messages for non-Deep tiers
- Halting the workflow due to QMD indexing failure
- Not proceeding to step-04 after this step

**Master Rule:** This step must NEVER fail the workflow. Use git as the authority for what belongs to the project. Always exclude `{alwaysExcluded}` and `{alwaysExcludedPatterns}`. Skip empty directories. Clean up stale collections. Index only user project directories with `**/*` mask. Non-Deep tiers skip silently.

- Remove all dot-directories (any directory starting with `.`)

**Store the result as `{user_dirs}`.**

### 3. Filter Empty Directories

For each directory in `{user_dirs}`, check if it contains any files:

Run: `find {dir_path} -type f -maxdepth 3 | head -1`

- If output is empty (no files found within 3 levels): **remove from `{user_dirs}`**
- If output has a result: keep in `{user_dirs}`

This prevents creating QMD collections for empty directories.

### 4. Evaluate Indexability

**If `{user_dirs}` is empty** (no user content directories with files found):

- Store in context: `{qmd_indexed: false, qmd_skip_reason: "no_project_content"}`
- Note for step-04: "No project source directories found to index. QMD collections will be created automatically on next [SF] run after adding project content."
- Proceed directly to section 7 (Auto-Proceed)

**If `{user_dirs}` has entries:** Continue to section 5.

### 5. Clean Up Stale Collections (Re-run Only)

Check for existing QMD collections that belong to this project but no longer map to a valid directory.

Run: `qmd collection list` and identify collections whose name starts with `{project_name}-`.

For each such collection:
- Extract the directory name from the collection name (the part after `{project_name}-`)
- Check if that directory still exists in `{user_dirs}`
- If NOT found in `{user_dirs}`: remove the stale collection with `qmd collection remove {collection_name}`
- If found: it will be updated in section 6

**Error handling:** If collection listing or removal fails, log and continue — stale cleanup is best-effort.

### 6. Index User Directories with QMD (Deep Tier Only)

For **each directory** in `{user_dirs}`, create a QMD collection:

- **Collection name**: `{project_name}-{sanitized_dirname}`
  - Sanitize the dirname: strip any leading underscores or dots (e.g., `_src` → `src`, `.config` → `config`)
  - Examples: `myapp-src`, `myapp-docs`, `myapp-lib`
- **Path**: the full absolute path to the directory
- **Mask**: `**/*` (captures all file types — source code, markdown, configs, etc.)

**Command per directory:**
```bash
qmd collection add {dir_path} --name {project_name}-{sanitized_dirname} --mask "**/*"
```

**Re-run handling:** If QMD reports "already exists" for a collection:
- Do NOT fail — this means it was indexed on a prior run
- Run `qmd update` once at the end to re-index all existing collections with fresh content
- Note the collection as "updated" rather than "created"

**After all collections are processed:**
- Store in context: `{qmd_indexed: true, qmd_collections: [list of names], qmd_total_files: total}`
- Count total files indexed across all collections from QMD output

**Timeout handling:** If indexing takes excessively long on a large project:
- Log that indexing is in progress but may need more time
- Note in context that index may be incomplete
- Do NOT halt or fail the workflow

**Error handling:** If QMD indexing fails for a specific directory:
- Log the specific error for that directory
- Continue with remaining directories — do not abort the loop
- Note which collections succeeded and which failed

If ALL directories fail:
- Store in context: `{qmd_indexed: false, qmd_skip_reason: "all_indexing_failed"}`
- Note that indexing can be retried by re-running [SF]
- The forge-tier.yaml already records `qmd: true` — the tool is available even if indexing failed

### 7. Auto-Proceed

"**Proceeding to forge status report...**"

#### Menu Handling Logic:

- After indexing completes (or is skipped), immediately load, read entire file, then execute {nextStepFile}

#### EXECUTION RULES:

- This is an auto-proceed step with no user choices
- Proceed directly to next step after indexing or skip

## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE

ONLY WHEN the tier check has been performed (and indexing completed or skipped accordingly) will you load and read fully `{nextStepFile}` to execute the report step.

---

## 🚨 SYSTEM SUCCESS/FAILURE METRICS

### ✅ SUCCESS:

- Git repo: `git ls-files` used to discover indexable directories (respects all .gitignore rules)
- Non-git: fallback exclusion list applied correctly
- `{alwaysExcluded}` directories excluded in ALL cases (git or non-git)
- `{alwaysExcludedPatterns}` patterns excluded in ALL cases (e.g., `_skf-learn` matched by `_*-learn`)
- Empty directories skipped — no collections created for dirs with no files
- Stale collections from prior runs detected and removed
- Collection names sanitized (leading `_` and `.` stripped from dirname portion)
- Deep tier with user content: each user directory indexed as its own QMD collection with `**/*` mask
- Deep tier with no user content: skipped with informative note for step-04
- Deep tier re-run: stale collections removed, existing updated via `qmd update`, new directories added
- Quick/Forge tier: skipped silently with no negative messaging
- Workflow continues regardless of indexing outcome
- Auto-proceeded to step-04

### ❌ SYSTEM FAILURE:

- Indexing directories matching `_*-learn` pattern
- Creating collections for empty directories

- Indexing the entire project root as a single collection (pollutes results with module internals)
- Not using `git ls-files` when the project IS a git repo

- Attempting QMD indexing for Quick or Forge tiers
- Displaying "skipped" or "missing" messages for non-Deep tiers
- Halting the workflow due to QMD indexing failure
- Not proceeding to step-04 after this step

**Master Rule:** This step must NEVER fail the workflow. Use git as the authority for what belongs to the project. Always exclude `{alwaysExcluded}` and `{alwaysExcludedPatterns}`. Skip empty directories. Clean up stale collections. Index only user project directories with `**/*` mask. Non-Deep tiers skip silently.

name: 'step-03-auto-index'
description: 'Smart-index user project directories as QMD collections (Deep tier only)'

nextStepFile: './step-04-report.md'

# Directory always excluded — module internals, never useful for project search
alwaysExcluded: _bmad

# Fallback exclusion list for non-git projects only
# Used ONLY when the project is not a git repository
fallbackExcludedDirs:
  - _bmad
  - node_modules
  - .git
  - forge-data
  - dist
  - build
  - coverage
  - __pycache__
  - target
  - .next
  - .nuxt
  - .cache
  - vendor
  - out
  - .turbo
  - venv
  - .venv
  - .output
---

# Step 3: Smart Auto-Index Project

## STEP GOAL:

If the detected tier is Deep, discover **user project directories** and index each as a targeted QMD collection. Uses `git ls-files` as the authoritative source for what belongs to the project (respects all `.gitignore` rules), with a hardcoded fallback for non-git projects. The `_bmad/` directory is **always** excluded regardless of git status.

For Quick and Forge tiers, skip silently and proceed.

## MANDATORY EXECUTION RULES (READ FIRST):

### Universal Rules:

- 📖 CRITICAL: Read the complete step file before taking any action
- 🔄 CRITICAL: When loading next step, ensure entire file is read
- 🎯 Execute all operations autonomously — no user interaction

### Role Reinforcement:

- ✅ You are a system executor performing smart conditional indexing
- ✅ Graceful degradation is paramount — never fail the workflow over indexing
- ✅ No negative messaging — do not mention what non-Deep tiers are missing

### Step-Specific Rules:

- 🎯 Focus only on targeted QMD indexing (Deep tier) or graceful skip (other tiers)
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to display "missing" or "skipped" messages for non-Deep tiers
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to fail the workflow if QMD indexing encounters errors
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to index `_bmad/` — always excluded, non-negotiable
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to index the entire project root as a single collection
- 💬 If indexing fails: log the issue, note that index can be retried, continue

## EXECUTION PROTOCOLS:

- 🎯 Follow the MANDATORY SEQUENCE exactly
- 💾 QMD indexing targets only user project directories — never module internals
- 📖 Use {calculated_tier} from step-01 context
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to attempt indexing for Quick or Forge tiers
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to use `**/*.md` as the mask — use `**/*` to capture all file types (source code, docs, configs)

## CONTEXT BOUNDARIES:

- Available: {calculated_tier} from step-01, forge-tier.yaml written in step-02
- Available: {project_name} from workflow config
- Focus: conditional QMD indexing only
- Limits: only index if Deep tier AND user content exists
- Dependencies: step-02 must have completed (forge-tier.yaml exists)

## MANDATORY SEQUENCE

**CRITICAL:** Follow this sequence exactly. Do not skip, reorder, or improvise.

### 1. Check Tier

Read `{calculated_tier}` from context.

**If tier is NOT Deep:** Proceed directly to section 5 (Auto-Proceed) — no output, no messaging.

**If tier IS Deep:** Continue to section 2.

### 2. Discover User Project Directories

Determine which top-level directories contain user project content that should be indexed.

#### 2a. Check if project is a git repository

Run: `git -C {project-root} rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree 2>/dev/null`

**If git repo (exit code 0):** Use the **git-aware strategy** (section 2b).

**If NOT a git repo:** Use the **fallback strategy** (section 2c).

#### 2b. Git-Aware Strategy (preferred)

Use `git ls-files` to discover which top-level directories contain tracked content. This automatically respects ALL `.gitignore` rules (project-level, nested, global, and `.git/info/exclude`).

Run:
```bash
cd {project-root} && git ls-files | cut -d/ -f1 | sort -u
```

This produces a list of top-level entries (files and directories) that git tracks.

**Filter the results:**
- Keep only **directories** (ignore root-level files — the agent can read those directly)
- Remove `{alwaysExcluded}` (`_bmad`) — it is tracked by git but must never be indexed for project search

**Store the result as `{user_dirs}`.**

#### 2c. Fallback Strategy (non-git projects)

List top-level directories in the project root:

Run: `ls -d {project-root}/*/ 2>/dev/null`

**Filter the results:**
- Remove all directories listed in `{fallbackExcludedDirs}`
- Remove all dot-directories (any directory starting with `.`)

**Store the result as `{user_dirs}`.**

### 3. Evaluate Indexability

**If `{user_dirs}` is empty** (no user content directories found):

- Store in context: `{qmd_indexed: false, qmd_skip_reason: "no_project_content"}`
- Note for step-04: "No project source directories found to index. QMD collections will be created automatically on next [SF] run after adding project content."
- Proceed directly to section 5 (Auto-Proceed)

**If `{user_dirs}` has entries:** Continue to section 4.

### 4. Index User Directories with QMD (Deep Tier Only)

For **each directory** in `{user_dirs}`, create a QMD collection:

- **Collection name**: `{project_name}-{dirname}` (e.g., `myapp-src`, `myapp-docs`, `myapp-lib`)
- **Path**: the full absolute path to the directory
- **Mask**: `**/*` (captures all file types — source code, markdown, configs, etc.)

**Command per directory:**
```bash
qmd collection add {dir_path} --name {project_name}-{dirname} --mask "**/*"
```

**Re-run handling:** If QMD reports "already exists" for a collection:
- Do NOT fail — this means it was indexed on a prior run
- Run `qmd update` once at the end to re-index all existing collections with fresh content
- Note the collection as "updated" rather than "created"

**After all collections are processed:**
- Store in context: `{qmd_indexed: true, qmd_collections: [list of names], qmd_total_files: total}`
- Count total files indexed across all collections from QMD output

**Timeout handling:** If indexing takes excessively long on a large project:
- Log that indexing is in progress but may need more time
- Note in context that index may be incomplete
- Do NOT halt or fail the workflow

**Error handling:** If QMD indexing fails for a specific directory:
- Log the specific error for that directory
- Continue with remaining directories — do not abort the loop
- Note which collections succeeded and which failed

If ALL directories fail:
- Store in context: `{qmd_indexed: false, qmd_skip_reason: "all_indexing_failed"}`
- Note that indexing can be retried by re-running [SF]
- The forge-tier.yaml already records `qmd: true` — the tool is available even if indexing failed

### 5. Auto-Proceed

"**Proceeding to forge status report...**"

#### Menu Handling Logic:

- After indexing completes (or is skipped), immediately load, read entire file, then execute {nextStepFile}

#### EXECUTION RULES:

- This is an auto-proceed step with no user choices
- Proceed directly to next step after indexing or skip

## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE

ONLY WHEN the tier check has been performed (and indexing completed or skipped accordingly) will you load and read fully `{nextStepFile}` to execute the report step.

---

## 🚨 SYSTEM SUCCESS/FAILURE METRICS

### ✅ SUCCESS:

- Git repo: `git ls-files` used to discover indexable directories (respects all .gitignore rules)
- Non-git: fallback exclusion list applied correctly
- `_bmad/` excluded in ALL cases (git or non-git)
- Deep tier with user content: each user directory indexed as its own QMD collection with `**/*` mask
- Deep tier with no user content: skipped with informative note for step-04
- Deep tier re-run: existing collections updated via `qmd update`, new directories added
- Quick/Forge tier: skipped silently with no negative messaging
- Workflow continues regardless of indexing outcome
- Auto-proceeded to step-04

### ❌ SYSTEM FAILURE:

- Indexing `_bmad/` under any circumstance
- Using `**/*.md` mask instead of `**/*` (misses source code files critical for skill generation)
- Indexing the entire project root as a single collection (pollutes results with module internals)
- Not using `git ls-files` when the project IS a git repo
- Attempting QMD indexing for Quick or Forge tiers
- Displaying "skipped" or "missing" messages for non-Deep tiers
- Halting the workflow due to QMD indexing failure
- Not proceeding to step-04 after this step

**Master Rule:** This step must NEVER fail the workflow. Use git as the authority for what belongs to the project. Always exclude `_bmad/`. Index only user project directories with `**/*` mask. Non-Deep tiers skip silently.

content.text:
All five changes applied. Here's a summary of what was added:

| Change | Section | Detail |
|--------|---------|--------|
| **Skip empty dirs** | New section 3 | `find -type f -maxdepth 3 \| head -1` check before creating collections |
| **Exclude `_*-learn`** | Frontmatter `alwaysExcludedPatterns` | Glob pattern applied in both git-aware and fallback strategies |
| **`forge-data` always excluded** | Moved to `alwaysExcluded` | No longer depends on fallback-only list — excluded even in git repos |
| **Stale collection cleanup** | New section 5 | On re-run, detects `{project}-*` collections that no longer map to existing dirs and removes them |
| **Sanitize collection names** | Section 6 | Strip leading `_` and `.` from dirname — `_src` → `myapp-src`, not `myapp-_src` |

Section numbering was updated (Auto-Proceed is now section 7). Ready to test again whenever you want.

content.thinking:
Let me analyze the output:

1. **Git detection** — correctly identified "not a git repo", used fallback strategy ✓
2. **`_bmad/` excluded** — ✓
3. **`forge-data/` excluded** — ✓ (now always excluded, not just fallback)
4. **`_skf-learn/` excluded** — matched by `_*-learn` pattern ✓
5. **Stale collection cleanup** — checked for `titi-` prefixed collections, found none ✓
6. **`skills/` kept** — correctly identified as user directory ✓


7. **Empty directory NOT skipped** — there's a mismatch: the agent detected `.gitkeep` as content and created a collection, but QMD filtered it out and reported zero files, resulting in an empty collection being created anyway.

The real issue is that the emptiness check passes when it finds `.gitkeep` (since it's technically a file), but QMD doesn't count placeholder files. The fix is to exclude `.gitkeep` and similar convention files like `.keep` from the find command so the check accurately reflects whether a directory has actual content.

content.text:
The execution is much cleaner. All five changes are working:

- `_bmad/`, `forge-data/`, `_skf-learn/` all correctly excluded
- Stale collection check ran (no prior collections found)
- Pattern matching worked (`_skf-learn` matched by `_*-learn`)

**One issue:** `skills/` passed the empty directory check because it contains `.gitkeep` — a placeholder file that git uses to track empty directories. QMD then created a collection with 0 indexable files. The empty check was defeated by `.gitkeep`.

Quick fix — update section 3 to exclude placeholder files from the emptiness check:

For each directory in `{user_dirs}`, check if it contains any meaningful files (not just git placeholder files):

Run: `find {dir_path} -type f -maxdepth 3 -not -name '.gitkeep' -not -name '.keep' -not -name '.placeholder' | head -1`

- If output has a result: keep in `{user_dirs}`

This prevents creating QMD collections for directories that only contain placeholder files like `.gitkeep`.

description: 'Smart-index user project directories as QMD collections (Deep tier only)'

nextStepFile: './step-04-report.md'

# Directories ALWAYS excluded regardless of git status — module internals and forge artifacts
alwaysExcluded:
  - _bmad
  - forge-data

# Directory name patterns ALWAYS excluded (glob-style matching)

  - '_*-learn'

# Fallback exclusion list for non-git projects only
# Used ONLY when the project is not a git repository
fallbackExcludedDirs:
  - _bmad
  - forge-data
  - node_modules
  - .git
  - dist
  - build
  - coverage
  - __pycache__
  - target
  - .next
  - .nuxt

  - out

  - venv
  - .venv
  - .output
---

# Step 3: Smart Auto-Index Project

## STEP GOAL:

If the detected tier is Deep, discover **user project directories** and index each as a targeted QMD collection. Uses `git ls-files` as the authoritative source for what belongs to the project (respects all `.gitignore` rules), with a hardcoded fallback for non-git projects.

Directories in `{alwaysExcluded}` and patterns in `{alwaysExcludedPatterns}` are excluded regardless of git status. Empty directories are skipped. Stale collections from prior runs are cleaned up.

For Quick and Forge tiers, skip silently and proceed.

## MANDATORY EXECUTION RULES (READ FIRST):

### Universal Rules:

- 📖 CRITICAL: Read the complete step file before taking any action
- 🔄 CRITICAL: When loading next step, ensure entire file is read
- 🎯 Execute all operations autonomously — no user interaction

### Role Reinforcement:

- ✅ You are a system executor performing smart conditional indexing
- ✅ Graceful degradation is paramount — never fail the workflow over indexing
- ✅ No negative messaging — do not mention what non-Deep tiers are missing

### Step-Specific Rules:

- 🎯 Focus only on targeted QMD indexing (Deep tier) or graceful skip (other tiers)
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to display "missing" or "skipped" messages for non-Deep tiers
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to fail the workflow if QMD indexing encounters errors
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to index directories matching `{alwaysExcluded}` or `{alwaysExcludedPatterns}`
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to index the entire project root as a single collection
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to create collections for empty directories
- 💬 If indexing fails: log the issue, note that index can be retried, continue

## EXECUTION PROTOCOLS:

- 🎯 Follow the MANDATORY SEQUENCE exactly
- 💾 QMD indexing targets only user project directories — never module internals
- 📖 Use {calculated_tier} from step-01 context
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to attempt indexing for Quick or Forge tiers
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to use `**/*.md` as the mask — use `**/*` to capture all file types (source code, docs, configs)

## CONTEXT BOUNDARIES:

- Available: {calculated_tier} from step-01, forge-tier.yaml written in step-02
- Available: {project_name} from workflow config
- Focus: conditional QMD indexing only
- Limits: only index if Deep tier AND user content exists
- Dependencies: step-02 must have completed (forge-tier.yaml exists)

## MANDATORY SEQUENCE

**CRITICAL:** Follow this sequence exactly. Do not skip, reorder, or improvise.

### 1. Check Tier

Read `{calculated_tier}` from context.

**If tier is NOT Deep:** Proceed directly to section 7 (Auto-Proceed) — no output, no messaging.

**If tier IS Deep:** Continue to section 2.

### 2. Discover User Project Directories

Determine which top-level directories contain user project content that should be indexed.

#### 2a. Check if project is a git repository

Run: `git -C {project-root} rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree 2>/dev/null`

**If git repo (exit code 0):** Use the **git-aware strategy** (section 2b).

**If NOT a git repo:** Use the **fallback strategy** (section 2c).

#### 2b. Git-Aware Strategy (preferred)

Use `git ls-files` to discover which top-level directories contain tracked content. This automatically respects ALL `.gitignore` rules (project-level, nested, global, and `.git/info/exclude`).

Run:
```bash
cd {project-root} && git ls-files | cut -d/ -f1 | sort -u
```

This produces a list of top-level entries (files and directories) that git tracks.

**Filter the results:**
- Keep only **directories** (ignore root-level files — the agent can read those directly)
- Remove all directories matching `{alwaysExcluded}` (exact match)
- Remove all directories matching `{alwaysExcludedPatterns}` (glob match — e.g., `_*-learn` matches `_skf-learn`, `_xyz-learn`)

**Store the result as `{user_dirs}`.**

#### 2c. Fallback Strategy (non-git projects)

List top-level directories in the project root:

Run: `ls -d {project-root}/*/ 2>/dev/null`

**Filter the results:**
- Remove all directories listed in `{fallbackExcludedDirs}` (exact match)
- Remove all directories matching `{alwaysExcludedPatterns}` (glob match)
- Remove all dot-directories (any directory starting with `.`)

**Store the result as `{user_dirs}`.**

### 3. Filter Empty Directories

For each directory in `{user_dirs}`, check if it contains any files:

Run: `find {dir_path} -type f -maxdepth 3 | head -1`

- If output is empty (no files found within 3 levels): **remove from `{user_dirs}`**
- If output has a result: keep in `{user_dirs}`

This prevents creating QMD collections for empty directories.

### 4. Evaluate Indexability

**If `{user_dirs}` is empty** (no user content directories with files found):

- Store in context: `{qmd_indexed: false, qmd_skip_reason: "no_project_content"}`
- Note for step-04: "No project source directories found to index. QMD collections will be created automatically on next [SF] run after adding project content."
- Proceed directly to section 7 (Auto-Proceed)

**If `{user_dirs}` has entries:** Continue to section 5.

### 5. Clean Up Stale Collections (Re-run Only)

Check for existing QMD collections that belong to this project but no longer map to a valid directory.

Run: `qmd collection list` and identify collections whose name starts with `{project_name}-`.

For each such collection:
- Extract the directory name from the collection name (the part after `{project_name}-`)
- Check if that directory still exists in `{user_dirs}`
- If NOT found in `{user_dirs}`: remove the stale collection with `qmd collection remove {collection_name}`
- If found: it will be updated in section 6

**Error handling:** If collection listing or removal fails, log and continue — stale cleanup is best-effort.

### 6. Index User Directories with QMD (Deep Tier Only)

For **each directory** in `{user_dirs}`, create a QMD collection:

- **Collection name**: `{project_name}-{sanitized_dirname}`
  - Sanitize the dirname: strip any leading underscores or dots (e.g., `_src` → `src`, `.config` → `config`)
  - Examples: `myapp-src`, `myapp-docs`, `myapp-lib`
- **Path**: the full absolute path to the directory
- **Mask**: `**/*` (captures all file types — source code, markdown, configs, etc.)

**Command per directory:**
```bash
qmd collection add {dir_path} --name {project_name}-{sanitized_dirname} --mask "**/*"
```

**Re-run handling:** If QMD reports "already exists" for a collection:
- Do NOT fail — this means it was indexed on a prior run
- Run `qmd update` once at the end to re-index all existing collections with fresh content
- Note the collection as "updated" rather than "created"

**After all collections are processed:**
- Store in context: `{qmd_indexed: true, qmd_collections: [list of names], qmd_total_files: total}`
- Count total files indexed across all collections from QMD output

**Timeout handling:** If indexing takes excessively long on a large project:
- Log that indexing is in progress but may need more time
- Note in context that index may be incomplete
- Do NOT halt or fail the workflow

**Error handling:** If QMD indexing fails for a specific directory:
- Log the specific error for that directory
- Continue with remaining directories — do not abort the loop
- Note which collections succeeded and which failed

If ALL directories fail:
- Store in context: `{qmd_indexed: false, qmd_skip_reason: "all_indexing_failed"}`
- Note that indexing can be retried by re-running [SF]
- The forge-tier.yaml already records `qmd: true` — the tool is available even if indexing failed

### 7. Auto-Proceed

"**Proceeding to forge status report...**"

#### Menu Handling Logic:

- After indexing completes (or is skipped), immediately load, read entire file, then execute {nextStepFile}

#### EXECUTION RULES:

- This is an auto-proceed step with no user choices
- Proceed directly to next step after indexing or skip

## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE

ONLY WHEN the tier check has been performed (and indexing completed or skipped accordingly) will you load and read fully `{nextStepFile}` to execute the report step.

---

## 🚨 SYSTEM SUCCESS/FAILURE METRICS

### ✅ SUCCESS:

- Git repo: `git ls-files` used to discover indexable directories (respects all .gitignore rules)
- Non-git: fallback exclusion list applied correctly
- `{alwaysExcluded}` directories excluded in ALL cases (git or non-git)
- `{alwaysExcludedPatterns}` patterns excluded in ALL cases (e.g., `_skf-learn` matched by `_*-learn`)
- Empty directories skipped — no collections created for dirs with no files
- Stale collections from prior runs detected and removed
- Collection names sanitized (leading `_` and `.` stripped from dirname portion)
- Deep tier with user content: each user directory indexed as its own QMD collection with `**/*` mask
- Deep tier with no user content: skipped with informative note for step-04
- Deep tier re-run: stale collections removed, existing updated via `qmd update`, new directories added
- Quick/Forge tier: skipped silently with no negative messaging
- Workflow continues regardless of indexing outcome
- Auto-proceeded to step-04

### ❌ SYSTEM FAILURE:

- Indexing `_bmad/` or `forge-data/` under any circumstance
- Indexing directories matching `_*-learn` pattern
- Creating collections for empty directories
- Using `**/*.md` mask instead of `**/*` (misses source code files critical for skill generation)
- Indexing the entire project root as a single collection (pollutes results with module internals)
- Not using `git ls-files` when the project IS a git repo
- Leaving stale collections from deleted directories on re-run
- Attempting QMD indexing for Quick or Forge tiers
- Displaying "skipped" or "missing" messages for non-Deep tiers
- Halting the workflow due to QMD indexing failure
- Not proceeding to step-04 after this step

**Master Rule:** This step must NEVER fail the workflow. Use git as the authority for what belongs to the project. Always exclude `{alwaysExcluded}` and `{alwaysExcludedPatterns}`. Skip empty directories. Clean up stale collections. Index only user project directories with `**/*` mask. Non-Deep tiers skip silently.

+
+**Store the result as `{user_dirs}`.**
+
+#### 2c. Fallback Strategy (non-git projects)
+
+List top-level directories in the project root:
+
+Run: `ls -d {project-root}/*/ 2>/dev/null`
+

+- Remove all directories listed in `{fallbackExcludedDirs}` (exact match)
+- Remove all directories matching `{alwaysExcludedPatterns}` (glob match)

+

input.command:
git add src/workflows/setup-forge/steps-c/step-03-auto-index.md src/workflows/setup-forge/steps-c/step-04-report.md && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix: smart auto-index excludes module internals and indexes all file types

The QMD auto-index step was indexing the entire project root with **/*.md,
which polluted search results with 131 internal _bmad/ module files and
missed source code files critical for skill generation.

- Use git ls-files to discover indexable dirs (respects .gitignore)
- Fallback exclusion list for non-git projects
- Always exclude _bmad/, forge-data/, and _*-learn pattern dirs
- Use **/* mask instead of **/*.md to capture source code
- Per-directory QMD collections instead of single root collection
- Skip empty directories (ignore .gitkeep placeholders)
- Clean up stale collections on re-run
- Sanitize collection names (strip leading _ and .)
- Update step-04 report to show per-collection QMD index details

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

message.content: do we need to update @README.md or @website/src/content/docs or @_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/medium-article-skf.md?

content.thinking:
The user is asking whether the README.md, website docs, or the Medium article need to be updated based on the changes we made to step-03-auto-index.md.

Let me think about what changed and whether any of these documents reference the auto-indexing behavior:

1. **README.md** - I read it. Let me check if it mentions QMD indexing behavior, the `**/*.md` mask, or anything about what gets indexed. Looking at the README:
   - Line 86-87 mentions "Deep mode" and QMD: "Deep mode — add QMD (a local hybrid search engine). SKF indexes your project's markdown — docs, changelogs, issues, migration guides — into searchable collections."
   - This description is now inaccurate since we changed the file pattern from `**/*.md` to `**/*`, meaning it now indexes all file types, not just markdown files.
   - Line 140 about the `setup-forge` workflow remains correct.

2. **Medium article** - The same line 86 description appears there too, claiming QMD only indexes markdown files, which is no longer true after our changes.

3. **Website docs** - I need to check those as well, though they likely contain similar outdated references.

The core issue is that multiple places claim QMD indexes only markdown when it actually indexes all file types now. Beyond that, there are other behavioral changes worth noting: QMD now creates per-directory collections rather than a single project-root one, excludes module internals from indexing, skips empty directories, and handles git-related files differently.

The most critical documentation fix is updating the claim about markdown-only indexing across the README, Medium article, and website docs.

content.text:
Yes. The README and Medium article both describe QMD/Deep mode as indexing only "markdown" — that's now wrong since we changed the mask to `**/*` (all file types including source code).

Let me check the website docs too.
